finding.contract_claim: The schema field `confidence` declares numeric bounds (type number, minimum 0.0, maximum 1.0) — an obligation that consuming code must enforce when the value is provided.
finding.observed_behavior: No application code reads or validates a `confidence` configuration value against these bounds; the declaration appears only inside an LLM tool_use input_schema in tools.py, where the model itself is the consumer of the constraint.

finding.triage_reasoning: The `confidence` field is declared inside an LLM `tool_use` `input_schema`. In this architecture the schema is transmitted to the LLM API, which acts as the direct consumer of the `minimum`/`maximum` bounds — the model is constrained to emit a value in [0, 1]. There is no project-level obligation for application code to re-validate the LLM's output against these bounds; the contract lives at the LLM API layer. Reality of the gap fails: the schema IS consumed, just by the LLM rather than by host Python code. Dismiss.
